description_long: This is a traditional wooden water well featuring a hexagonal base constructed from dark, textured wood planks. The well is topped with a large, pitched wooden roof supported by four vertical posts, providing shelter from the elements. A hand-crank mechanism with a visible handle and gear is attached to one side, connected to a rope that descends into the well, suggesting a manual water-drawing system. The rope is thick and twisted, likely made of natural fiber, and is wound around a horizontal spindle. The interior of the well is open, allowing access to the water below, and the structure is robust, with no visible decorative elements. The overall design is utilitarian, focusing on function and durability, suitable for placement in parks, courtyards, or community spaces.
description: A sturdy wooden water well with a hexagonal base, pitched roof, and hand-crank rope mechanism. The design is functional and rustic, with no decorative features.
description_view_0: Front view showing the hand-crank mechanism, rope, and open well beneath the pitched roof.
description_view_1: Side view highlighting the hexagonal base and the full expanse of the wooden roof, with the crank mechanism not visible.
description_view_2: Rear view displaying the opposite side of the hand-crank, with the rope and spindle clearly visible.
description_view_3: Side view from the opposite angle, showing the back of the crank mechanism and the well's open interior.
attribute_well_cover_design: Large pitched wooden roof supported by four posts; no lid or slab directly covering the well opening.
attribute_presence_of_pulley_or_bucket_m: Manual hand-crank with rope and spindle mechanism, no visible bucket.
attribute_surrounding_protective_structu: Hexagonal wooden wall approximately waist-high, forming the well's base and acting as a barrier.
